title: Fretting corrosion resistance and fretting corrosion product cytocompatibility of ferritic stainless steel

The weight loss for SUS317L and FJ in a 96-h
continuous-fretting corrosion test at 0 mV (SCE) is
shown in Figure 5. The weight loss of Ti-6A1-4V alloy,
obtained under the same conditions after 72 h, is in-
cluded for comparison. Obviously the specimen was
anodically polarized at 0 mV (SCE), based on the re-
sults shown in Figure 2. The weight losses of both pins
and plates were increased in proportion to time. The
weight loss of FJ was larger than that of SUS317L. For
both FJ and SUS317L, the weight losses of pins were
larger than those of plates. The weight losses of these
two stainless steels were much smaller than those of
Ti-6A1-4V alloy. Because the electrochemically or me-
chanically attacked area during the fretting corrosion
test was mainly limited to the contact area of pin and
plate, the weight loss of the pin and plate by relative
electrochemical dissolution processes should be almost
the same. The difference in weight loss between pin
and plate was probably a result of mechanical interac-
